schools.summary: Christ withdrew to give the disciples room to do what he enjoined (Chrysostom); he passed from special teaching of the Twelve to general preaching in the cities, 'from heaven to earth,' a pattern for all preachers (Remigius).

The recurring formula 'when Jesus had ended' closes each of the five discourses; the verse belongs to the preceding chapter; diatassōn = detailed orders; he did not desist from teaching while the apostles laboured.

Henry: Christ's commissions imply commands; he gave full instructions and trained them by degrees; preaching he kept to himself as the more honourable, the labourers' increase an encouragement to diligence, not an excuse for negligence.

themes: withdrew to make room for the Twelve

schools.summary: Gregory/Jerome: John, knowing Christ, sent to ask whether he would descend to 'the world below' to redeem the waiting dead; Ambrose: John doubted not in faith but in love, unable to think the Coming One must die; Hilary: John could not err, and figures the Law sending men to the gospel.

Chrysostom rejects the 'world below' reading and the notion of John's ignorance: nearing death he feared his disciples would fall away, and sent them to be convinced by the works, for the testimony of deeds is stronger than words.
John imprisoned at Machaerus; Henry allows a mixture of unbelief even in true faith under long unrelieved trouble; Ellicott reads the sickness of deferred hope; the account parallels Lk 7:18-35.

Calvin calls it 'foolish' to think John doubted and 'absurd' the errand to the dead; the dying herald, fearing his disciples would fall away, sent them to Christ — pastors must direct disciples to Christ, the only Teacher (Jn 3:29; 2 Cor 11:2).

debates.question: Why did John send his disciples to Christ?

sides.position: for his disciples' sake, to fix their faith on Christ before his death
for John's own satisfaction / a mixture of unbelief under trial
to ask whether Christ would descend to redeem the dead in the world below
John doubted not in faith but in love, regarding Christ's death

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on receiving the cure as granted to faith (the blind men of ch. 9).

themes: art thou he that should come

schools.summary: 'He that should come' (ho erchomenos) a familiar Messianic title (Ps 118:26; Dan 7:13); Lightfoot refers it to John's imprisonment, Clarke to a hope of a secular kingdom; Ellicott reads impatience rather than doubt — the King seeming to desert his herald (Jer 20:7).
Calvin: the question glances at the disciples' sloth and shows the nature of faith — resting on God's truth, satisfied with Christ alone, not turned to another.

debates.question: What lay behind 'or do we look for another'?

sides.position: a hope of a speedy secular kingdom

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ's descent to redeem the righteous waiting from Adam — John among the herald-prophets — feeding the 'world below' reading.

themes: art thou he that should come

schools.summary: Christ answers by the works, not a bare claim — miracles wrought 'in his own name and by his own power'; judge men by their works (Clarke); the symbolical message for John to interpret; in that hour many were cured (Lk 7:21), the messengers eyewitnesses.

Calvin: an indirect reply, that the thing might speak for itself and afford fuller instruction; the miracles adapted by quotations from Isaiah 35 and 61, that the disciples go away with a reply 'clear and free from all ambiguity.'

The list fulfils Messianic prophecy (Isa 35:5; 61:1); each class of miracle already recorded; the climax 'the poor have the gospel preached to them' (Lk 4:18) the greatest spiritual note, the poor overlooked by all before Christ.
Clarke reads the cures as emblems of salvation — sinners blind, lame, leprous, deaf, dead — Christ redeeming from all; works the rabbis allowed the Messiah should perform.
Calvin: 'the poor' are the wretched and despised, whose poverty should animate courage to seek Christ — yet only those really poor, convinced of their poverty.

'Offended' = a stumbling-block; men stumbled at Christ's mean parentage, poor followers, strict doctrine; 'happy is he who shall not take offence' (Barnes); a tender rebuke to John's doubt given as a beatitude (Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: faith must contend with offences, for Christ is a rock of offence (1 Pet 2:8), appearing 'with his cross, disfigured and despised'; pronouncing the steadfast blessed leaves unbelievers no excuse; Clarke: by his meanness he condemns worldly pride.

Chrysostom: Christ speaks 'as they departed' to avoid flattery, and clears John of the fickleness the crowd suspected — proven by their own going out to the desert; John neither inconstant by nature nor by self-indulgence.

Gregory: the reed = the carnal mind swayed by praise or detraction, which John never was; luxury not innocent; Jerome: austere preaching shuns kings' courts, and reads desert/reed/raiment mystically; Augustine: blame not the use but the lust of things.
Henry: John 'a firm, resolute man... the same in all weathers,' no weather-cock testimony (Jn 1:20); Christ's question puts the hearers to account for their intentions in attending the word.

themes: a reed shaken with the wind
what went ye out to see

schools.summary: John's camel's-hair raiment showed he was 'dead to all the pomps of the world'; an argument from consistency — one so strange to a court's pleasures would not waver under a prison's terrors; the mortified are least likely driven off by persecution (Henry).

Gregory: luxury not innocent, else the Lord had not commended John's coarse dress (1 Pet 3:3); Jerome allegorizes the soft garment as a mind lost in self-indulgence and the 'kings' as fallen angels, lords of this world.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on receiving a cure as granted to faith.

schools.summary: Henry: John 'more than a prophet' — the forerunner not a king but a transcendent prophet who saw the day dawn and pointed to the Messiah present ('Behold the Lamb of God,' Jn 1:29), where the others saw him afar.
John greater by his nearness to Christ (sent 'before thy face') and by the added reward of baptizing his Lord (Jerome); the prophet foretells, but John showed the Christ as present (Gregory).
Christ, a competent Judge, owns John superior to all other prophets, John having owned himself inferior to Christ; the forerunner not a king, lest the kingdom seem laid in earthly power.

The text is Malachi 3:1; John prophesied of by the prophets, and so greater than they; a messenger on a great errand, deriving his honour from him whose messenger he was, to prepare Christ's way by discovering men's need of a Saviour.

John called 'angel' (messenger) not by nature but by the dignity of his office (Jerome); more than a prophet because he showed present what he had foretold (Gregory); the Gloss: he makes ready the way by preaching repentance and baptizing.

'Women, not virgins' — John set before all wedlock-born but not the Virgin-born Lord (Rabanus); the words make John equal to the saints, not above them (Jerome); Augustine offers two readings — the least angel in glory, or Christ himself greater though later-born.

John 'the mountain peak between the old and the new' (Robertson); the least in the kingdom surpasses him in privilege/knowledge — Ellicott takes this of the 'little ones' (Mt 10:42), rejecting the Christ-reading as a poor truism.

Calvin: nothing about personal rank, only pre-eminence of office — gospel ministers surpass John as he surpassed the prophets, holding out Christ as the finished, eternal satisfaction; Chrysostom guards against setting John above Christ.

debates.question: Who is 'he that is least in the kingdom of heaven'?

sides.position: the least gospel minister/believer, greater in privilege and knowledge

outliers.note: Treats baptism as the end of the OT and beginning of the NT, ranking John above Elias, Enoch, and Moses, yet below the Son.

themes: John the threshold of the new

schools.summary: Gregory: penitent sinners take the kingdom by violence, entering another's place; Jerome: great violence, the earth-born seeking heaven, obtaining by excellence what is not theirs by nature; Hilary: the Gentiles by might enter and hold Israel's glory.

The eager rush of crowds to John and Christ, like a besieged city stormed (Lightfoot, Barnes); the 'violent' the tax-gatherers and sinners seizing the proffered mercy (Clarke); Ellicott prefers the passive — the kingdom grasped as a prey (Jer 45:5), no hostile purpose.
Calvin: the Gospel that so suddenly gains hearts must be of God; faith leads men 'to rush forward as it were with a violent struggle' — yet only men of a particular class, the rest unmoved.

debates.question: Is the verb passive ('the kingdom is stormed') or middle ('forces its way')?

sides.position: passive — taken by force by eager seekers
either is admissible; the form is ambiguous
of the Gentiles taking Israel's glory by force

themes: the violent take it by force

schools.summary: The prophets and law prophesied of Messiah 'to come' under shadows; since John, vision and prophecy are sealed up; John introduced a new dispensation; the order inverted to stress the prophetic over the legislative (Ellicott).

Calvin: the Law represented God 'under figures and shadows as absent'; it is criminal to remain indifferent when God's presence is manifested; John's preaching was a part of the Gospel but 'little more than a first lesson.'

outliers.note: Stray fragment — baptism the end of the Old Testament and beginning of the New, John its first-fruit.

schools.summary: No transmigration: John is Elijah 'in the spirit and power of Elias' (Lk 1:17); the Jews expected the Tishbite in person (Mt 17:10); Clarke's three marks of Malachi's forerunner all fit John; he denied being Elijah in person (Jn 1:21) but Jesus affirms he is the promised Elijah (Mt 17:12).

Calvin: by 'if ye will receive it' Christ glances at their hardened obstinacy in shutting their eyes to the light; John's office does not depend on their approbation, but they are charged with ingratitude.

A formula used for weighty, hard sayings demanding 'more than ordinary powers of thought' (Ellicott; Mt 13:9); here the truths that John is more than a prophet, the Law ended, John is Elias; not all have spiritual ears (Gill).

Calvin: a mark of subjects not to be heard 'in an indifferent manner,' and a reminder why the mysteries are not received by all — many are deaf, hindered by their own unbelief and others' influence; the elect are exhorted to attend.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the baptismal anointing of the ears, citing the verse.

themes: he that hath ears to hear

schools.summary: The complaining children are 'this generation' itself — petulant, pleased by nothing, faulting John's austerity and Christ's freedom alike (Ellicott, Gloss); the markets a place of play, idleness, or business where the word is slighted.

Chrysostom keeps the inverted reading: John and Christ came 'opposite ways, to do the same thing,' like two hunters — so the generation has nothing whereof to accuse them; wisdom justified though nothing is effected in them.

Henry: God uses varied means — piping in promises, mourning in threatenings; sons of thunder and sons of consolation (1 Cor 12:6) — yet with many fruitless; the perverse reflect on the ministers by whom they enjoy the means.
Hilary reads the children as the prophets inviting Israel to confession by song; Christ, the Wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24), is justified of her children — those who seize the kingdom by faith.

debates.question: Who are the children that pipe and mourn?

sides.position: the fickle generation itself, complaining of both ministries
John and Christ, inviting a sullen generation that will not respond

themes: whereunto shall I liken this generation

schools.summary: Jewish children mimicking the weddings and funerals of their elders — 'we have piped... we have begun the music' for the dance, and 'mourned' (ekopsasthe, beat the breast, Lk 23:48; cf. Mt 9:23) — expecting responses that did not come; the merry pipe answering to Christ, the dirge to John.

outliers.note: Long fragment on John's rebuke of Herod and the dance of Herodias's daughter — belongs to the beheading narrative, not the piping parable; a stray.

themes: we piped and ye danced not
we mourned and ye lamented not
children's game of wedding and funeral

schools.summary: 'Neither eating nor drinking' = John ate sparingly (locusts and honey), shunning feasts and sociable converse, his life answering to the mournful dirge; 'he hath a devil' a common charge of insanity ascribed to possession (Jn 7:20; 8:48), the diet of a madman.

Clarke/Wesley: a sinner will rather blame holiness than imitate it; John's austerity, agreeing with his doctrine, ought to have worked on them, yet was calumniated as 'melancholy, from an evil spirit.'

themes: John came neither eating nor drinking

schools.summary: Christ shared 'the common life of man' (Cana, Matthew's feast), recalling the cavil 'why eat with publicans and sinners' (Lk 5:30); 'gluttonous, winebibber' echoes Deut 21:20 against one who pleased not himself (Rom 15:3); philos 'friend' a malicious nickname turned name of honour — 'the sinner's lover' (Robertson; Lk 15:2).
Augustine: the children who justify wisdom are the Apostles, who knew the kingdom is not meat and drink but patient enduring (Phil 4:12).

Henry: Christ is Wisdom, the saints the children given him (Heb 2:13), who comply with his grace and so evidence his wisdom (Lk 7:29); Ellicott widens it — true seekers welcome holiness in any real form.

debates.question: 'Justified of her children' or 'of her works'?

sides.position: of her children — the wise/believers who vindicate wisdom
of her works — vindicated by results (the best texts here)

outliers.note: Stray fragment quoting Daniel 7:13, the Son of Man coming with the clouds.

themes: the Son of man came eating and drinking

schools.summary: Christ 'began' only now to upbraid, having first invited; the sin is impenitence under the fullest light (Clarke, Robertson); the rebuke parallels Lk 10:13, likely uttered more than once; the design of his miracles was to invite men to repentance (Calvin).

Jerome states and answers the hard question — if Tyre/Sidon would have repented, the fault seems his who did not preach; answer: we know not God's judgments, Christ bound not to leave Judea; Augustine presses it into predestination — what they 'might have' done withheld where not given (Jn 12:40); Chrysostom: Bethsaida gave apostles, making the charge heavier.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the Samaritans as intermediate between Jews and Gentiles.

themes: why not preach to Tyre and Sidon

schools.summary: 'Woe' is 'alas,' a compassionate declaration, not an imprecation (Clarke, Wesley); idolatrous Tyre and Sidon would have repented in sackcloth and ashes; men are judged by their opportunities (Ellicott; Rom 2); the second-class condition states what 'would have' been (Robertson).

Calvin: the comparison deepens the impression on Jews who abhorred Tyre and Sidon — more hope from the irreligious than from hardened Judea; reasoning of what they 'would have done' from the facts, not the secret decree; warns against thorny questions, bidding reverence before the height and depth of God's judgments.

A settled truth against the cities' self-flattery that they were heaven's favourites and the Tyrians execrable; 'the day of judgment' a usual Jewish phrase; at that tribunal the benefits received, as well as the sins, will overwhelm the impenitent (Clarke).
Wesley: the degree of their misery greater than Tyre and Sidon, yea Sodom — graded retribution by the means of grace abused.

themes: more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon
means of grace into the account

schools.summary: Capernaum 'exalted to heaven' by Christ's presence and miracles (Ellicott lists them); 'brought down to hell' = Hades, a state of desolation as a city, literally fulfilled in its ruin (Barnes, Clarke), though future punishment also threatened; even Sodom would have remained (Jude 7); 'perhaps' leaves freedom of choice (Remigius).

Calvin: the most-favoured city, as filthy as if no grace had touched it, faces the more dreadful punishment in proportion to its favours; the profanation of God's gifts never passes unpunished, yet God remains a just avenger.

themes: exalted to heaven, brought to hell

schools.summary: Clarke: 'the land of Sodom' = its ancient inhabitants, suffering eternal fire (Jude 7); more tolerable for them than for gospel-rejecters — various degrees of punishment in hell by degrees of guilt; the 'day of judgment' both the temporal ruin and the final (Rev 20:14).

Sodom has much to answer for but not the sin of neglecting Christ, as Capernaum will; the gospel, rejected, becomes a savour of death unto death (2 Cor 2:16); gospel-age professors the greatest debtors to mercy or to justice.

'I confess' = 'I praise Thee, not I accuse Myself' (Augustine); 'babes' the humble; 'Lord of heaven and earth' against the Arians (Jerome); Gregory: condemns pride not understanding; the lesson is humility before the counsels of heaven.

Chrysostom: the 'wise' are such only in seeming, excluded by their pride (Rom 10:3); Christ joys not in their loss but that the babes have known what the wise have not; one of six ways he leads them to faith.

Calvin: the doctrine of free election — only God's good pleasure causes the diversity; he chooses the weak and foolish (1 Cor 1:27) that human wisdom not obscure the praise of grace; Henry: thanksgiving the answer to dark thoughts; Barnes: 'so seemed good' the only satisfactory reason, for God never errs.

Ellicott and Robertson note the prayer is Johannine in spirit and language, a rare recorded glimpse of Christ at worship; 'thank' better 'praise'; the 'wise and prudent' the scribes wise in conceit, the 'babes' the teachable disciples.

themes: hid from the wise, revealed to babes

schools.summary: The diversity owing not to natural sagacity, worthiness, or foreseen use, but to God's good pleasure only (Gill) — a reason 'better than ten thousand'; Ellicott hears an echo of the baptism and Nativity, the only such recorded prayer outside John.

Calvin: God's will is 'the highest reason and justice'; we yield it with difficulty, plunging into the depth and gnashing our teeth, but Christ acquiesces — 'whatever God has determined must be regarded as right'; men storm because 'God should have the last word.'

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the scrip and staff of the mission charge (ch. 10).

schools.summary: 'Committed' admits nothing human — he was Lord of all at his begetting; the mutual knowledge proves the Son of one substance with the Father (Chrysostom, Hilary); the Father revealed by the Son/Word, which in declaring declares itself (Augustine); against Eunomius, knowing 'by equality' differs from knowing by His revealing (Jerome).

A sublime claim not to be whittled down; the timeless aorist points to a moment in eternity, implying pre-existence and the Messianic consciousness (Robertson; Mt 28:18); 'knoweth' = full knowledge; a denial of the Tempter's claim (Lk 4:6).

Calvin joins it to election — the anxious are bid come direct to Christ, the channel through which hidden salvation flows, received by faith; 'it is lost time to seek him elsewhere,' for in him dwells the fulness (Col 2:9).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the angels beholding God only as far as they can bear, the Son and Spirit alone knowing the Father fully.

themes: none knoweth the Son but the Father

schools.summary: 'All whosoever are in trouble' — not to punish but to remit and refresh (Chrysostom); humility the foundation, love making the burden easy (Augustine); the labouring under the Law and the sins of the world called (Hilary); the burden of sin heavy (Jerome; Ps 38:4).

'Labour' active, 'heavy laden' passive suffering; the 'I' emphatic — rest none else can give (Ellicott); vv.28-30 special treasures of Matthew, the rest a 'rejuvenation' (Robertson); the primary reference to those under the ceremonial law (Acts 15:10) and chiefly the burdened sinner (Barnes, Clarke).

Calvin restricts the call to those overwhelmed by sin and ready to sink — not the self-righteous nor the worldly; the fainting prepares them for grace, itself the gift of the Spirit; the rest is the free pardon of sins that alone gives peace.

outliers.note: Stray fragment ranging the one Spirit's titles — Comforter, Spirit of Truth, Spirit of the Father.

themes: come unto me all ye that labour

schools.summary: The phrase Rabbinical — 'the yoke of the kingdom of heaven'; the yoke is Christ's religion and teaching (Barnes, Ellicott), the lightest ever imposed, so that religion is freedom (Jn 8:36); the rabbis used 'yoke' for school, and Jesus made humility a virtue (Robertson; Phil 2:3); the rest quoted from Jer 6:16.

Calvin: the rest is on condition of the yoke — not exemption from the warfare of the flesh but training under discipline; the mention of meekness is to form us to imitate Christ, since the flesh shrinks from his yoke until conformed to him; Wesley: whoever finds not rest is not meek and lowly.

themes: learn of me, meek and lowly

schools.summary: 'Easy' = chrēstos, good/kind/profitable, a yoke 'lined with love' (Henry); easy not to nature but to the regenerate with the Spirit's help and God's love (Gill); proved in experience — peace, joy, reward (Barnes); the 'burden' the new commandment of love (Ellicott; 1 Jn 5:3).

Reconciles the 'narrow way' (Mt 7:14): the narrow entrance is 'made broad by the unspeakable sweetness of love' (Rabanus); the Gospel seeks the will, commanding what we can do (Jerome); Clarke sums the yoke as receiving Christ as Messiah and being conformed to his Word and Spirit.
